factual-accuracy.requirement: States combined federal+Ontario marginal tax rate at $95k income is 31%-32% or identifies bracket approximately $93k-$106k
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the combined federal+Ontario marginal tax rate at CAD 95,000 is approximately 43.4%, and it does not identify an approximately $93k-$106k bracket or a 31%-32% combined rate. Therefore it fails to provide the required marginal rate range or bracket approximation for that income level.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates $1,000 RRSP contribution saves approximately $310-$320 in tax at 31%-32% marginal rate
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses RRSP tax savings using a 43.4% marginal rate and gives an example of $17,100 saving about $7,421, but it never calculates that a $1,000 RRSP contribution saves approximately $310–$320 at a 31%–32% marginal rate. The specific $1,000-at-31%–32% example is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit is $32,490
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses RRSP limits for 2026 (e.g., CAD 33,810) and provides an example contribution room of CAD 17,100, but it never states that the 2025 RRSP annual dollar limit is $32,490. The specific figure $32,490 is absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RRSP contribution room is 18% of prior year earned income (lesser of percentage or dollar limit plus carry-forward)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states the RRSP deduction limit is the lesser of 18% of the previous year's earned income or a dollar maximum, and it also notes unused contribution room can be carried forward indefinitely.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates 18% of $95,000 yields approximately $17,000-$17,100 in new RRSP contribution room
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly calculates 0.18 × 95,000 = CAD 17,100 and presents this as the RRSP contribution room amount, which falls within the required ~$17,000–$17,100 range.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG provides 20% grant on first $2,500 contributed per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that the Canada Education Savings Grant (CESG) provides 20% on the first CAD 2,500 contributed per year per beneficiary, which matches the required statement.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG annual maximum is $500 per child per year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states that CESG is “20% on the first CAD 2,500 contribution per year, resulting in a maximum of CAD 500 CESG per beneficiary annually,” which matches the required annual maximum of $500 per child per year.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states “Lifetime CESG maximum per beneficiary: CAD 7,200” in the RESP section, which matches the requirement that the CESG lifetime maximum is $7,200 per child.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains $5,000 contribution per child in one year can capture up to $1,000 CESG when unused grant room carried forward
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states that unused CESG room can be carried forward and that catch-up contributions of up to $5,000 per year can attract up to $1,000 of CESG. This directly explains how a $5,000 contribution in one year can capture up to $1,000 when unused grant room exists.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States to receive CESG at ages 16-17 must have contributed either $2,000 before year child turned 15 OR $100 in any four prior years
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses CESG annual limits, catch-up contributions, and eligibility up to age 17, but it does not state the specific rule that to receive CESG at ages 16–17 the child must have had $2,000 contributed before turning 15 or $100 contributed in any four prior years. That exact eligibility condition is missing.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States RESP lifetime contribution limit is $50,000 per beneficiary
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under the RESP section that the “Lifetime Contribution Limit” is “CAD 50,000 per child,” which is equivalent to $50,000 per beneficiary.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Identifies $36,000 in contributions needed to maximize $7,200 CESG (since $7,200 divided by 20% equals $36,000)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response mentions the $7,200 lifetime CESG maximum but never calculates or states that $36,000 of RESP contributions are needed to maximize it (i.e., $7,200 ÷ 20%). It discusses annual $2,500 contributions and catch-up rules without providing the required $36,000 figure.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes up to $14,000 additional contribution per child ($50,000 lifetime minus $36,000 for CESG) earns tax-deferred growth without additional grant
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the RESP $50,000 lifetime contribution limit and the $7,200 lifetime CESG maximum, but it does not note that up to $14,000 additional contributions per child (i.e., $50,000 minus $36,000) can still earn tax-deferred growth without additional grant. It also never mentions the $36,000 CESG-maximizing contribution amount or explicitly contrasts grant-eligible vs non-grant-eligible contributions.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States contributor claims RRSP deduction immediately at their marginal rate whether contribution goes to own RRSP or spousal RRSP
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes that contributing to a spousal RRSP “yields tax deductions at your higher marginal rate,” but it does not explicitly state that the RRSP deduction is claimed immediately at the contributor’s marginal rate regardless of whether the contribution goes to their own RRSP or a spousal RRSP. It also doesn’t clearly contrast that the immediate deduction treatment is the same in both cases.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP account is owned by spouse and withdrawals after attribution period are taxed to spouse not contributor
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response notes the three-year attribution rule and implies withdrawals after that window are taxed to the spouse, but it does not explicitly explain that the spousal RRSP account is owned by the spouse. It also does not clearly state that withdrawals after the attribution period are taxed to the spouse (not the contributor) in a direct, unambiguous way.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States spousal RRSP 3-calendar-year attribution rule (withdrawal in contribution year or following 2 calendar years attributed back to contributor)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly mentions the spousal RRSP “three-year attribution rule” and explains that withdrawals within 3 years of contribution are taxed back to the contributor, matching the contribution year plus following two calendar years concept.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Notes minimum RRIF payments from spousal account are exempt from attribution (only excess over minimum can be attributed back)
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response discusses the three-year attribution rule for spousal RRSP withdrawals but does not mention RRIF minimum payments or that minimum RRIF withdrawals from a spousal account are exempt from attribution (with only amounts above the minimum attributed). This specific RRIF attribution exception is entirely absent.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains spousal RRSP creates income-splitting opportunity if spouse expected to be in lower tax bracket during retirement
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explains that a spousal RRSP is beneficial when the spouse’s current or expected retirement income is significantly lower, allowing deductions at the contributor’s higher marginal rate and withdrawals taxed in the spouse’s lower retirement bracket, creating income-splitting.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States 2025 TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states “Annual Contribution Limit 2026: CAD 7,000” for the TFSA, but it never states that the 2025 TFSA annual contribution limit is $7,000. The required year (2025) is not addressed explicitly or implicitly.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States TFSA withdrawals are tax-free and do not affect income-tested benefits
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response says TFSA withdrawals are “tax-free” and discusses recontribution timing, but it does not state that TFSA withdrawals do not affect income-tested benefits. The specific impact on income-tested benefits (e.g., CCB/GIS) is not addressed.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Explains withdrawn amount from TFSA is added back to contribution room on January 1 of following calendar year
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states under TFSA “Withdrawals and Recontributions” that amounts withdrawn in one year are added back to contribution room on January 1 of the following calendar year, and reiterates this again in the TFSA mechanics section.

factual-accuracy.requirement: Calculates $2,500 per child equals $5,000 total annual RESP contribution for two children to maximize $1,000 combined annual CESG
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response explicitly states an RESP target of “Min. CAD 5,000 total ($2,500 per child)” for two children, which equals $5,000 annually and is tied to maximizing CESG (i.e., $500 per child, $1,000 combined). This satisfies the required calculation and purpose.

factual-accuracy.requirement: States combined marginal rate at $95k income is 37% or higher without clarifying this applies only to income above approximately $109k
factual-accuracy.explanation: The response states a combined marginal tax rate of approximately 43.4% at CAD 95,000 income and does not clarify that such a high combined marginal rate would apply only above roughly CAD 109k. This appears in section 2.1 and is used again to compute RRSP tax savings.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Identifies income threshold of approximately $106,000 where marginal rate increases above current 31-32% bracket to 33.89%
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses a marginal tax rate around 43.4% at CAD 95,000 but does not identify an income threshold near $106,000 where the marginal rate rises from a 31–32% bracket to 33.89%. It never mentions $106,000 or 33.89%, nor does it describe that specific bracket transition.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Calculates $14,000 non-CESG contribution capacity per child by subtracting $36,000 CESG-eligible from $50,000 lifetime limit
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response states the $50,000 lifetime RESP contribution limit and discusses CESG rules, but it never calculates a $14,000 non-CESG contribution capacity per child or performs the subtraction of $36,000 CESG-eligible from $50,000. The specific $36,000 figure and resulting $14,000 calculation are absent.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects $2,000-before-age-15 RESP requirement to children's current ages of 8 and 11 to avoid losing 16-17 grants
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response discusses CESG eligibility up to age 17 and notes the children are 8 and 11, but it does not mention the specific rule requiring $2,000 of RESP contributions before age 15 to qualify for CESG at ages 16–17. It also does not connect that $2,000-before-15 requirement to the children’s current ages to avoid losing 16–17 grants.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Provides specific monthly dollar contribution amounts for RESP RRSP and TFSA accounts (e.g. $417/month for two-child RESP)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response provides specific monthly dollar amounts in the allocation table, including RESP at CAD 417 per child (CAD 833 total), RRSP at about CAD 1,425/month, and TFSA at about CAD 583/month.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Analyzes that spousal RRSP provides minimal benefit when spouses expected to have similar retirement income levels
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly notes that when spouses have similar or equal incomes, the income-splitting advantage of a spousal RRSP diminishes and individual RRSP contributions may be equally effective. This directly analyzes that a spousal RRSP provides minimal benefit under similar expected retirement income levels.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Explains TFSA room restoration on January 1 enables withdrawal during slow months and recontribution when cash flow improves
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explains that TFSA withdrawals create new contribution room on January 1 of the following year and frames TFSA as a buffer for freelance income variability, enabling withdrawals in low-income months and later recontribution when cash flow improves (with caution about same-year recontributions).

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Connects 2025 RRSP contribution room calculation to 2024 earned income (approximately $17,100 from $95,000 at 18%)
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response explicitly ties RRSP contribution room to the prior year’s earned income by stating the limit is 18% of the previous year’s earned income and calculating 0.18 × 95,000 = CAD 17,100, which corresponds to 2025 room based on 2024 income.

breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.requirement: Recommends maximizing registered account contributions without addressing the stated 6-month emergency fund liquidity requirement
breadth-and-depth-of-analysis.explanation: The response repeatedly emphasizes maintaining a 6‑month emergency fund in liquid assets (often within a TFSA) and even states to prioritize building the emergency fund before maximizing RRSP contributions. While it suggests using full RRSP room and RESP targets, it does not recommend maximizing registered contributions without addressing the liquidity requirement.

presentation-quality.requirement: Uses precise Canadian tax terms (RRSP RESP TFSA CESG RRIF attribution marginal rate) without generic language
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses multiple precise Canadian tax terms throughout, including RRSP, TFSA, RESP, CESG, Ontario marginal tax rate, and the spousal RRSP attribution rule. These are used in context rather than generic language.

presentation-quality.requirement: Organizes multi-account strategy with headings numbered sections or tables for clarity
presentation-quality.explanation: The response is organized with numbered headings and subheadings (Sections 1–7) and includes a table summarizing annual and monthly contribution targets, providing clear multi-account strategy structure.

presentation-quality.requirement: Maintains professional financial planning tone without product sales marketing language or vague recommendations
presentation-quality.explanation: The response uses a formal, advisory financial planning style and focuses on tax rules, contribution limits, and allocation guidance. It does not include promotional or sales-oriented language for specific products, and most recommendations are framed as concrete targets (e.g., RESP $2,500/child, RRSP room) rather than purely vague suggestions.

presentation-quality.requirement: States a definitive recommended strategy for the RRSP-RESP-TFSA allocation question rather than only listing options
presentation-quality.explanation: The response provides a clear recommended allocation strategy, including a prioritized plan (emergency fund first, then RESP to capture CESG, then RRSP, then TFSA) and a specific hybrid RESP approach. It goes beyond listing options by stating concrete contribution targets and sequencing as the recommended strategy.

presentation-quality.requirement: Explains fundamental concepts like what an RRSP or TFSA is to a financially sophisticated asker who already understands these accounts
presentation-quality.explanation: The response includes basic definitional explanations of RRSP, TFSA, and RESP mechanics (e.g., contribution limits, withdrawal rules, what CESG is) in sections 1.1–1.3 and 4.2, rather than assuming the asker already understands these accounts.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites canada.ca (Canada Revenue Agency) for RRSP RESP TFSA rules contribution limits and program details
citation-quality.explanation: The response includes a Canada.ca (CRA) citation for TFSA contribution room calculation, but it does not cite Canada.ca/CRA sources for RRSP or RESP rules, contribution limits, or program details. RRSP and RESP information is sourced from third-party sites (e.g., Sun Life, TD, Waypoint) rather than CRA. Therefore, the required Canada.ca citations for RRSP and RESP are missing.

citation-quality.requirement: References authoritative source for 2025 Ontario marginal tax rates (accounting firms provincial budget documents or established Canadian tax sites)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites an established Canadian tax site for Ontario marginal tax rates via “Ontario Marginal Tax Rates 2026 | TaxTips.ca” in the Sources section. This constitutes an authoritative reference for Ontario marginal tax rates (though it is for 2026 rather than explicitly 2025).

citation-quality.requirement: Cites authoritative Canadian tax resource for spousal RRSP attribution rules and timing (e.g. taxtips.ca major accounting firms or CRA publications)
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites TaxTips.ca for Ontario marginal tax rates and discusses the spousal RRSP three-year attribution rule and timing, meeting the requirement for an authoritative Canadian tax resource reference.

citation-quality.requirement: Cites US tax authority sources (IRS.gov or US tax code provisions) as applicable to Canadian tax planning
citation-quality.explanation: The response cites only Canadian sources (e.g., Canada.ca/CRA) and Canadian financial institutions, and it does not reference IRS.gov or any US tax code provisions. All tax planning discussion is framed around Canadian accounts (RRSP/TFSA/RESP) and Ontario tax rates.
